description: The #MeToo movement exploded after the Harvey Weinstein revelations in October 2017. Initial near-universal support (~70%+ saying it was a good thing) gradually fractured as the movement expanded, with concerns about due process and 'going too far' creating a partial backlash by mid-2018.

key_events.description: Harvey Weinstein allegations break (NYT/New Yorker, October 2017). Alyssa Milano's #MeToo tweet goes viral (12M posts in 24 hours). Rapid cascade of accusations across industries.
Time Magazine names 'The Silence Breakers' as Person of the Year. Golden Globes 'Time's Up' campaign. Senate seats lost (Al Franken resignation). Peak support and cultural impact.
Aziz Ansari story (January 2018) sparks debate over 'where to draw the line.' Atlantic's 'The Humiliation of Aziz Ansari' divides feminists. Due process concerns surface.
Brett Kavanaugh confirmation hearings (September 2018). #MeToo becomes overtly partisan. 'Believe all women' vs. due process framing. Gender gap in support widens.

Pew (September 2018): 51% say #MeToo has been good, down from 58% six months prior. Among Republicans, 'gone too far' rises to 54%. Movement matures into institutional reforms (HR policies, legal changes).

calibration_notes: Initial surge then partial backlash pattern. Pew tracked: Feb 2018 — 58% said increased focus on harassment was positive; Sept 2018 — 51%. NPR/Marist found similar decline. Key dynamic: broad consensus on egregious cases (Weinstein) fractured as movement expanded to ambiguous situations and became politically coded. Gender and partisan gaps widened over time. Tests: consensus fracturing under scope expansion, partisan capture of social movements, 'too far' backlash dynamics, elite defection effects.
